input.answer: In my last internship, our checkout API kept timing out under load. I was asked to bring p95 latency under 200ms. I added a Redis cache for product lookups and batched the database writes. After the change p95 dropped from 800ms to about 120ms and the timeouts stopped.

input.answer: So, I think the project I want to talk about is, well, there were a lot of things going on, and honestly it is a bit hard to summarize because we kept changing direction. Basically the team wanted to build some kind of dashboard, and I was sort of involved in a few parts of it, like the frontend mostly but also a little backend here and there. We used a bunch of tools, you know, the usual stack, and there were meetings almost every day which took up a lot of time. I remember we had some issues with deadlines and people were stressed, and I tried to help where I could, fixing small things and reviewing some pull requests when I had time. In the end the thing kind of shipped, more or less, although there were still bugs and we never really finished all the features we originally planned.
Um, so like, I, you know, worked on this thing, it was, um, a website I guess, and like, yeah, it was okay I think.

input.answer: When our team CI pipeline kept failing because of flaky tests, I took ownership of stabilizing it. I went through the failing suites, quarantined the unreliable tests, and added proper waits instead of fixed sleeps. After that the pipeline became reliable and people stopped ignoring red builds.

note: built a real app with tech -> concrete=true (rule c)
